interactions.summary: Jiang continues Marmeladov's speech and interprets Orthodox God as merciful, knowing, and finally redeeming because humans suffer from the mysteries of the heart and cannot be wise about themselves.
He summarizes Protestant, Catholic, and Orthodox differences: chosen sin versus born-in-sin versus born-to-sin; few saved, all potentially saved, or humans beyond saving except by God.

Returning to Raskolnikov, Jiang reads his justification to Sonia and explains that revolutionary/Napoleonic reasoning can sound correct but collapses when it conflicts with the heart; later Raskolnikov confesses even when escape appears possible.

speaker_notes.note: Single-speaker lecture interwoven with quoted novel passages; no student questions in this packet.

claims.claim: Jiang defines Orthodox Christianity as fatalistic and merciful: humans do not know themselves and are beyond self-salvation, so only a wise forgiving God can redeem them.

Jiang contrasts Protestant salvation by elect wealth/work, Catholic forgiveness through obedience, and Orthodox dependence on God's mercy because the heart is too dark and mysterious for human redemption.
Raskolnikov's theory is a reasoned lawgiver doctrine: the strong who dare most become right in others' eyes, but Dostoevsky shows this reasoning violates the heart.
claims.temporal_scope: Crime and Punishment interpretation in this lecture.

claims.claim: Jiang says Nietzsche and Freud later read Crime and Punishment in their own ways, with Nietzsche seeing Raskolnikov's failure as insufficient belief in his own theory rather than the theory's falsity.
claims.temporal_scope: Forward-looking intellectual-history note in this lecture.

signature_moments.moment: Orthodox optimism is fatalistic because God knows what the heart cannot know.
signature_moments.source_phrase: it's fatalistic. But at the same time. It's also optimistic.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It preserves Jiang's central paradox: Russian darkness does not cancel mercy.

signature_moments.moment: God is great because only God can save what is beyond salvation.
signature_moments.source_phrase: only God. Can save. And redeem us.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It compresses Jiang's Orthodox theology into a sentence useful for the public read.

signature_moments.moment: When reason and the heart conflict, the heart wins.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This is the philosophical center of the Dostoevsky section.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: For Jiang, a fatalistic and merciful Christianity where humans are born to sin and only God can redeem the mysterious heart.
Marmeladov's daughter in Crime and Punishment, associated here with suffering, pity, and Raskolnikov's confession.

chronology_notes.note: Jiang flags Nietzsche and Freud as future interpretive consequences of Dostoevsky, saying the class will discuss this next time.

uncertainty_notes.note: ASR inserts periods between most words in quoted passages; semantic reading relies on Jiang's surrounding interpretation.
